text: Tito reportedly to talk with high-level Chinese Communists

text:
2. Tito reportedly to talk with high-level Chinese Communists:

Yugoslav diplomats in India and Burma have been notified by Belgrade that Tito will hold talks in the Yugoslav embassy
token_text: 2 tito reportedly to talk with high-level chinese communists yugoslav diplomats in india and burma have been notified by belgrade that tito will hold talks in the yugoslav embassy

text:
in Rangoon with high-level Chinese officials.
Economic exchanges with the Viet Minh will also be discussed by trade officials in Tito's party.

Comment: Tito would probably welcome an opportunity during his current trip to India and Burma to meet with Chinese representatives.

Yugoslavia was one of the first non-Orbit countries to offer to establish relations with Peiping, but Peiping has never accepted the offer. Belgrade has repeatedly maintained that Western recognition of the Peiping regime is desirable to ensure against Soviet domination. Tito has publicly stated, however, that as far as Yugoslavia is concerned the initiative for the establishment of normal relations must now come from Peiping.

It is believed that Peiping would agree to establish diplomatic relations with Yugoslavia, in line with the current Communist effort to improve relations with Belgrade.

Trade talks with the Viet Minh might lead to establishment of Yugoslav-Viet Minh diplomatic relations, which the Yugoslavs attempted in early 1950

text:
5. Chinese Communist radar facilities increased:

The number of coastal radar sites in Communist China, almost all of World War II type, has increased from 90 to 132 since July. This increase is not considered indicative of imminent military operations, but does indicate an improvement in Chinese Communist air warning capability for dealing with violations of frontier security.

It is possible that the introduction of newer Soviet radar types in the western USSR has freed more of the older sets for use in the Far East.

Comment: The growing number of radar sites in Communist China has coincided with a gradual redispositioning of jet fighters to bases from which they can react more quickly to overflights of the mainland coast. Added evidence that the Chinese Communists are improving their air defenses was revealed recently when two tracking nets began using a new and more accurate system of describing the locations of aircraft detected by radar.

In addition to the expanding belt of radar cover along the China coast, there is evidence that the Chinese have installed sets in the interior
